metadata.title: AFO Capability Blueprint
metadata.description: L1/L2 capability decomposition of the Agentic Front Office reference architecture. Single source of truth for capability assessment, platform mapping, and maturity diagnostics.

assessment_schema.description: Per-client maturity assessment overlay. Empty in template, populated during Sprint engagements.

fields.current_stage: 1-4 or null. Facilitator-rated current maturity based on stage indicators.
fields.target_stage: 1-4 or null. Agreed target state, typically Stage 3 for 18-month programs.
fields.notes: Free text. Facilitator observations, evidence, or client-stated context.
fields.assessed: Boolean. Whether this capability has been reviewed in the assessment.

layers.name: Experience layer
layers.design_question: How do customers, employees, and AI agents interact with the commercial engine?

l1_components.name: Conversational touchpoints
l1_components.description: AI-native interfaces where customers and agents engage through natural language and multimodal interaction

l2_capabilities.name: Multi-modal experience agent
l2_capabilities.description: AI-powered interfaces engaging customers across text, voice, image, and video modalities within a single conversation

adobe.notes: Emerging conversational capabilities through Agent Orchestrator; not a primary chat/voice platform

salesforce.notes: Agentforce provides native conversational agent framework across text and voice

sap.notes: Joule provides conversational AI but limited multi-modal experience composition compared to native CX platforms.

microsoft.notes: Copilot Studio provides multi-modal conversational agent framework across Microsoft ecosystem

google.notes: Strong conversational AI through Dialogflow CX with Gemini multimodal capabilities

aws.notes: Conversational AI through Lex with Bedrock Agents for multi-modal interactions; Connect for voice

anthropic.notes: Claude provides advanced multi-modal conversational AI across text, image, and code

openai.notes: Full multi-modal agent across text, voice, image, and video with native capabilities

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Basic chatbot with scripted responses
maturity_indicators.stage_2: LLM-powered conversational agent, single modality
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Multi-modal agent with context continuity across modalities
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Fully autonomous experience agent adapting modality to customer preference in real time

l2_capabilities.name: Conversational commerce agent
l2_capabilities.description: AI agent enabling product discovery, comparison, configuration, and transaction completion through conversational interfaces

adobe.notes: Commerce platform exists but conversational commerce is emerging, not mature

salesforce.notes: Commerce platform with emerging conversational commerce through Agentforce

sap.notes: Commerce Cloud supports conversational commerce via integration; not natively agentic.

microsoft.notes: Commerce platform with emerging Copilot-assisted shopping; conversational commerce still developing

google.notes: Conversational interfaces possible; no native commerce transaction handling

aws.notes: Conversational capabilities through Bedrock; no native commerce transaction platform

anthropic.notes: Can power conversational commerce through API integration; not a standalone commerce agent

openai.notes: Strong conversational commerce via Assistants API with function calling and retrieval

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Product FAQ bot
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Recommendation agent with catalog integration
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Full conversational commerce with cart, pricing, and checkout
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous commerce agent handling negotiation and custom pricing

l2_capabilities.name: Autonomous service agent
l2_capabilities.description: AI agent resolving service inquiries, processing claims, and executing service actions without human intervention for Tier-1 and select Tier-2 scenarios

adobe.notes: No native service agent platform; partner required (Salesforce, ServiceNow)

salesforce.notes: Strong autonomous service agent capabilities through Agentforce built on Service Cloud

sap.notes: Service Cloud handles case automation; Joule adds AI assist but autonomous agent capabilities are early.

microsoft.notes: Copilot agents for service with autonomous resolution capabilities

google.notes: Contact Center AI with autonomous agent capabilities and live agent handoff

aws.notes: Connect is enterprise contact center with AI-powered autonomous resolution through Bedrock Agents

anthropic.notes: Strong autonomous agent capabilities with tool use and extended thinking

openai.notes: Autonomous service agent capabilities through function calling and code interpreter

maturity_indicators.stage_1: FAQ deflection with human escalation
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Intent-based routing with partial autonomous resolution
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Full autonomous resolution for defined categories with governed escalation
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Proactive service agent detecting and resolving issues before customer contact

l2_capabilities.name: Machine workflow agent
l2_capabilities.description: Automated process agents executing structured workflows as part of larger agentic chains

adobe.notes: No native RPA/workflow automation agent; partner required

salesforce.notes: Workflow automation through Flow and MuleSoft; not purpose-built agentic workflow agents

sap.notes: SAP Build Process Automation handles machine workflows; agent-native patterns emerging.

microsoft.notes: Strong workflow automation through Power Automate with AI-powered process agents

google.notes: Cloud-native workflow orchestration; not purpose-built for agentic workflow agents

aws.notes: Serverless workflow orchestration; not purpose-built agentic workflow agents

anthropic.notes: Native support for machine-to-machine workflows via API and tool use

openai.notes: Native function calling and tool use for machine-to-machine workflows

maturity_indicators.stage_1: RPA bots on fixed workflows
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Workflow agents triggered by agentic signals
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Adaptive workflow agents modifying execution based on context
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Self-optimizing workflow agents redesigning processes based on outcomes

l1_components.name: Traditional channels
l1_components.description: Established digital and physical channels delivering experiences to human customers and employees

l2_capabilities.description: Website and web application experiences including CMS-delivered pages, landing pages, and portals

salesforce.notes: Portal and community sites; not a full CMS for marketing websites

sap.notes: Commerce Cloud provides full headless and storefront web experience management.

microsoft.notes: Basic portal/site capabilities through Power Pages; not a full enterprise CMS

google.notes: No CMS platform; partner required

aws.notes: No CMS platform; partner required

maturity_indicators.stage_2: CMS-managed site with basic personalization
maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-personalized web experiences with real-time content adaptation and agent-assisted interactions
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous web experiences that self-optimize layout, content, and interaction patterns per visitor

l2_capabilities.name: Email
l2_capabilities.description: Outbound and triggered email across marketing, transactional, and service contexts

adobe.notes: Full email capabilities across journey-triggered and campaign-based

salesforce.notes: Full email capabilities across campaign and journey-triggered

sap.notes: Emarsys is a strong email marketing and automation platform with deep personalization.

microsoft.notes: Email capabilities within Dynamics Marketing; less depth than dedicated email platforms

google.notes: No email marketing platform; partner required

aws.notes: Email delivery through SES; campaign email through Pinpoint; not a full marketing email platform

maturity_indicators.stage_2: Triggered emails with basic dynamic content
maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-generated email content with send-time optimization and cross-journey coordination
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous email agents composing and optimizing messages in real time per recipient

l2_capabilities.name: SMS / Push
l2_capabilities.description: Mobile messaging for alerts, notifications, and conversational engagement

adobe.notes: Native SMS and push through Journey Optimizer

salesforce.notes: SMS and push messaging through Marketing Cloud

sap.notes: Emarsys provides native SMS, push notification, and mobile messaging capabilities.

google.notes: No SMS/push platform; partner required

aws.notes: Push and SMS through Pinpoint; limited marketing automation depth

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Manual SMS blasts with no personalization
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Triggered SMS/push with basic segmentation
maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-optimized mobile messaging with cross-channel coordination and conversational capability
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous mobile engagement agents adapting channel, timing, and content to individual preference

l2_capabilities.name: Paid media
l2_capabilities.description: Programmatic advertising, social media advertising, and paid search activation

adobe.notes: Strong activation to paid media through CDP audience syndication

salesforce.notes: Audience activation to paid media; not a media buying platform

sap.notes: Emarsys supports paid media audience syndication; not a full media buying platform.

microsoft.notes: No native paid media activation; partner required

google.notes: Industry-leading programmatic and search advertising through Google Marketing Platform

aws.notes: No paid media platform; partner required

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Manual media buying with broad targeting
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Programmatic buying with basic audience segments
maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-optimized media activation with real-time bidding, suppression, and cross-channel budget allocation
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous media agents continuously optimizing spend across platforms against commercial outcomes

l2_capabilities.name: Search & display
l2_capabilities.description: Organic search optimization, GEO readiness, and display advertising delivery

adobe.notes: Strong SEO tooling through AEM; GEO optimization emerging but not yet a distinct product capability

salesforce.notes: No native SEO/GEO optimization; partner required

sap.notes: No native search and display advertising product. Relies on partner integrations.

microsoft.notes: No native SEO/GEO optimization; partner required

google.notes: Dominant search platform; strong SEO tools; emerging GEO considerations through AI Overviews

aws.notes: No SEO/GEO optimization; partner required

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Basic SEO with manual optimization
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Data-driven SEO with keyword and content optimization
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Integrated search strategy spanning SEO, GEO, and display with AI-driven content optimization
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous search presence management optimizing for both traditional and AI-mediated discovery

l2_capabilities.name: Events & experiential
l2_capabilities.description: In-person and virtual event experiences integrated into the commercial engine

adobe.notes: Event registration and follow-up through Marketo; not a dedicated event platform

salesforce.notes: Event-adjacent marketing automation; not a dedicated event platform

sap.notes: No native events and experiential marketing platform.

microsoft.notes: Teams-based virtual events with marketing follow-up; not a dedicated event platform

google.notes: No event management; partner required

aws.notes: No event management; partner required

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Standalone events with no digital integration
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Events with basic registration and follow-up automation
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Events integrated into the commercial engine with AI-driven attendee engagement and post-event nurture
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous event orchestration agents managing personalized experiences across physical and virtual formats

l2_capabilities.name: Print & physical
l2_capabilities.description: Direct mail, in-store, and physical collateral produced and governed through ICO

adobe.notes: Can produce print-ready assets; not a dedicated print production platform

salesforce.notes: No print/physical production capability

sap.notes: No native print and physical channel management.

microsoft.notes: No print/physical production capability

google.notes: No print/physical production capability

aws.notes: No print/physical production

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Generic printed materials produced manually
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Template-based print with basic variable data
maturity_indicators.stage_3: ICO-governed print production with AI-personalized content and brand compliance
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous print production triggered by journey events with fully personalized physical collateral

l1_components.name: Agent-to-agent interfaces
l1_components.description: Machine-to-machine interfaces where AI agents interact without human presence on either side

l2_capabilities.name: API commerce endpoints
l2_capabilities.description: Structured APIs enabling buyer AI agents to discover products, request quotes, and execute transactions programmatically

adobe.notes: Commerce APIs exist but not yet designed for agent-to-agent transaction protocols

salesforce.notes: Commerce APIs through Commerce Cloud; not yet designed for autonomous agent transactions

sap.notes: Commerce Cloud exposes OCC APIs; Integration Suite provides API management. Not purpose-built for agent commerce.

microsoft.notes: Commerce APIs exist; not designed for agent-to-agent protocols

google.notes: API management through Apigee; not purpose-built for agent-to-agent commerce

aws.notes: API management through API Gateway; not purpose-built for agent-to-agent commerce

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Standard product APIs for human-facing apps
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Machine-readable catalog and pricing APIs
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Full agent-commerce APIs supporting negotiation and dynamic pricing
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous agent-to-agent commerce with multi-party negotiation

l2_capabilities.name: Agent protocol endpoints
l2_capabilities.description: MCP, A2A, and ACP protocol endpoints enabling external agents to discover capabilities, exchange context, and request actions

adobe.notes: MCP support emerging through Agent Orchestrator; A2A/ACP support TBD

salesforce.notes: ACP protocol support emerging; MuleSoft for API integration

sap.notes: No native agent protocol endpoint support (A2A, MCP). Early exploration via SAP Business AI.

microsoft.notes: Plugin architecture and tool calling; MCP and multi-protocol support emerging

google.notes: Google developed the A2A protocol; native support through Agentspace

aws.notes: Tool use and action groups in Bedrock Agents; multi-protocol support (MCP, A2A, ACP) still emerging

anthropic.notes: API-based agent protocol support; emerging MCP protocol for agent interoperability

openai.notes: API-based agent endpoints; emerging agent protocol support

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No agent protocol support
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic MCP tool endpoints for internal agents
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Multi-protocol support for internal and partner agents
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Full agent interoperability with discovery, negotiation, and governance across organizational boundaries

layers.name: Autonomous experience orchestration (AEO)
layers.design_question: How does the commercial engine compose unified experiences from functional capabilities in real time?
layers.design_principle: Orchestrate across functions, not within them

l1_components.name: Orchestration core
l1_components.description: Central orchestration capabilities coordinating signals, decisions, and actions across the commercial engine

l2_capabilities.name: Multi-agent coordination
l2_capabilities.description: Orchestration of multiple specialized agents on shared goals: task decomposition, sequencing, and result synthesis across platform boundaries

adobe.notes: Emerging multi-agent coordination through Agent Orchestrator; cross-platform orchestration still developing

salesforce.notes: Native multi-agent coordination within Salesforce ecosystem through Agentforce

sap.notes: Joule is single-agent; no multi-agent coordination framework yet.

microsoft.notes: Multi-agent orchestration through Copilot Studio with Semantic Kernel for custom coordination

google.notes: Multi-agent orchestration through Agentspace with Vertex AI agent framework

aws.notes: Multi-agent orchestration through Bedrock Agents with Step Functions for complex workflows

anthropic.notes: Multi-agent coordination through tool use, parallel processing, and extended thinking

openai.notes: Multi-agent coordination via Assistants API with parallel function calling

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No multi-agent coordination
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Intra-platform coordination within single vendor
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Cross-platform coordination with protocol-mediated handoffs
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous multi-agent networks with self-organizing task allocation

l2_capabilities.name: Agentic conversation pattern
l2_capabilities.description: Patterns managing how agents converse: request-response, pub-sub, saga, and choreography across platforms

adobe.notes: Agentic patterns supported within Adobe ecosystem; cross-platform conversation patterns require integration

salesforce.notes: Agentic patterns within Salesforce; cross-platform conversation patterns require MuleSoft

sap.notes: Joule supports conversational patterns but not agentic conversation orchestration across agents.

microsoft.notes: Strong agentic patterns through Semantic Kernel with enterprise messaging infrastructure

google.notes: A2A protocol designed specifically for agent conversation patterns; native support

aws.notes: Agent patterns within Bedrock; cross-platform conversation protocols still developing

anthropic.notes: Advanced conversational patterns with context window up to 200K tokens

openai.notes: Advanced conversational patterns with 128K context and structured outputs

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Direct API calls between systems
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Event-driven patterns within platform
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Protocol-based patterns across platforms (MCP, A2A, ACP)
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Adaptive patterns that self-optimize based on workflow performance

l2_capabilities.name: Agentic process flow
l2_capabilities.description: Definition and execution of multi-step agentic workflows spanning functions and platforms with branching, error handling, and escalation

adobe.notes: Strong journey/process flow capabilities within Adobe ecosystem

salesforce.notes: Strong agentic process flow through Agentforce + Flow automation framework

sap.notes: SAP Build provides process flow; Joule adds AI-driven steps. Not fully agentic orchestration.

microsoft.notes: Agentic flow definition through Copilot Studio + Power Automate process orchestration

google.notes: Agentic process flows through Agentspace with Cloud Workflows for orchestration

aws.notes: Step Functions provides enterprise workflow orchestration with Bedrock Agent integration

anthropic.notes: Can orchestrate process flows via API; requires integration layer for full process automation

openai.notes: Can orchestrate process flows via function calling; requires integration layer

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Manual workflow configuration per platform
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Platform-native agentic flows within single ecosystem
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Cross-platform flows with governed handoffs and escalation
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Self-designing process flows optimizing based on outcome data

l2_capabilities.name: Human-AI balance
l2_capabilities.description: Dynamic management of boundary between autonomous action and human involvement: escalation triggers, confidence thresholds, progressive autonomy

adobe.notes: Human approval steps in journeys; progressive autonomy framework still emerging

salesforce.notes: Human-in-the-loop controls and escalation built into Agentforce framework

sap.notes: Human-in-the-loop supported in SAP Build workflows; Joule provides escalation. Basic balance controls.

microsoft.notes: Human-in-the-loop controls and escalation built into Copilot Studio framework

google.notes: Human-in-the-loop controls within Agentspace; framework still maturing

aws.notes: Human approval through Step Functions; progressive autonomy framework not yet formalized

anthropic.notes: Built-in human oversight patterns, constitutional AI alignment

openai.notes: Moderation API and content filtering; less emphasis on human-in-the-loop patterns

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Fixed rules for human vs. automated
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Confidence-based escalation within functions
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Dynamic autonomy tiers adjusting based on performance and context
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Self-calibrating autonomy earning trust through demonstrated outcomes

l2_capabilities.name: Event-driven routing
l2_capabilities.description: Real-time signal capture, enrichment, and routing to agents or systems based on event type, customer context, and commercial value

adobe.notes: Real-time event collection and routing is core AEP capability; streaming profiles and event-driven activation native

salesforce.notes: Real-time event-driven architecture through Platform Events and Data Cloud streaming

sap.notes: Event Mesh provides event-driven architecture; routing is infrastructure-level, not experience-aware.

microsoft.notes: Enterprise-grade event routing through Azure messaging and streaming infrastructure

google.notes: Enterprise-grade event streaming through Pub/Sub with real-time processing via Dataflow

aws.notes: Enterprise event-driven architecture through EventBridge with Kinesis for streaming

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Batch-processed event handling
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Real-time routing within platform
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Cross-platform enrichment and fan-out with Commercial Brain context
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Predictive routing anticipating signals before they occur

l1_components.name: Experience composition
l1_components.description: Capabilities assembling coordinated responses from multiple functions into unified customer experiences

l2_capabilities.name: Journey orchestration
l2_capabilities.description: Continuous, signal-driven orchestration of customer journeys across channels and functions in real time rather than campaign cycles

adobe.notes: Industry-leading journey orchestration with real-time decisioning and cross-channel activation

salesforce.notes: Journey Builder for marketing journeys; not cross-functional orchestration across sales/service/commerce

sap.notes: Emarsys provides mature journey orchestration with multi-channel automation and AI-driven triggers.

microsoft.notes: Journey orchestration within Dynamics Marketing; less mature than dedicated journey platforms

google.notes: No journey orchestration platform; partner required (Adobe AJO, Braze)

aws.notes: Basic journey orchestration through Pinpoint; not a full cross-functional journey platform

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Campaign-based journey sequences
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Signal-triggered journeys within marketing
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Cross-functional journey orchestration spanning all pillars
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous journey design creating and optimizing based on outcome patterns

l2_capabilities.name: Dynamic segmentation
l2_capabilities.description: Real-time audience segmentation updating continuously from behavioral signals and agent-generated insights

adobe.notes: Real-time segmentation with streaming profile updates is core CDP capability

salesforce.notes: Real-time segmentation through Data Cloud with activation across channels

sap.notes: Emarsys and CDP provide robust dynamic segmentation with real-time audience computation.

microsoft.notes: Segmentation through Customer Insights; real-time streaming segmentation less mature

google.notes: Audience building through GA4 + BigQuery; not a real-time streaming segmentation platform

aws.notes: Audience segmentation through Pinpoint; real-time streaming segmentation less mature

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Static rule-based segments updated periodically
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Behavioral segments in near-real-time
maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-driven micro-segments with continuous cross-functional refinement
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Segment-of-one with predictive intent modeling

l2_capabilities.name: Personalization & next-best-action
l2_capabilities.description: Cross-functional decisioning determining optimal next action based on intent, context, commercial value, and journey stage

adobe.notes: Decisioning engine in AJO plus Target personalization; strong NBA capabilities

salesforce.notes: Einstein NBA across sales, service, and marketing with Agentforce decisioning

sap.notes: Emarsys provides next-best-action and personalization; Commerce Cloud adds product-level personalization.

microsoft.notes: Copilot-powered recommendations within Dynamics; cross-functional NBA less mature

google.notes: ML models for recommendations through Vertex AI; no native cross-functional NBA engine

aws.notes: Recommendation engine through Personalize; cross-functional NBA requires custom build

anthropic.notes: Can power personalization logic; requires data platform for customer context

openai.notes: Can power personalization and NBA logic; requires customer data platform

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Rule-based recommendations
maturity_indicators.stage_2: ML-driven NBA within single function
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Cross-functional NBA considering all pillars simultaneously
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous decisioning with real-time optimization and self-learning

l2_capabilities.description: Intelligent selection and presentation of offers based on customer context, margin guardrails, and competitive positioning

adobe.notes: Offer decisioning is a core AJO capability with constraint-based optimization

salesforce.notes: Offer management within marketing; cross-functional offer optimization limited

sap.notes: Basic offer optimization via Emarsys campaigns and Commerce promotions. Not ML-driven optimization.

microsoft.notes: Basic offer management; not a dedicated offer decisioning engine

google.notes: No offer decisioning engine; partner required

aws.notes: No offer decisioning engine; partner required

maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-optimized with real-time margin enforcement

l2_capabilities.description: Continuous testing and optimization across channels including A/B testing, multivariate testing, and automated adaptation

salesforce.notes: A/B testing within marketing; cross-channel experimentation less mature than dedicated testing platforms

l2_capabilities.name: Product recommendation
l2_capabilities.description: AI-driven product and content recommendations based on behavioral signals, purchase history, and commercial objectives

adobe.notes: Recommendation engine across web, commerce, and email touchpoints

salesforce.notes: Product recommendations in commerce; cross-channel recommendation less mature

layers.name: Functional pillars
layers.design_question: How do specialized commercial functions execute with both human expertise and agentic capabilities?

l1_components.name: Marketing
l1_components.description: Demand generation, brand management, campaign execution, and generative engine optimization

adobe.notes: Unified customer profiles with real-time behavioral data; strong audience intelligence through CJA

salesforce.notes: Unified customer profiles through Data Cloud with marketing audience intelligence

sap.notes: SAP CDP provides unified customer profiles; Emarsys adds audience intelligence and segmentation.

openai.notes: Can synthesize audience insights from data; not a customer data platform

layers.name: Governance & trust
layers.design_question: How does the organization ensure that every agent action is governed, auditable, and aligned with business and regulatory requirements?

l1_components.name: Autonomy & escalation management
l1_components.description: Frameworks defining where agents operate autonomously and where human oversight is required

l2_capabilities.name: Autonomy tier definition & enforcement
l2_capabilities.description: Policy framework defining agent autonomy levels (fully autonomous, supervised, human-approval-required) per action type, risk level, and domain

adobe.notes: Emerging autonomy controls within Agent Orchestrator; comprehensive tier management requires enterprise governance framework

salesforce.notes: Autonomy tiers and guardrails built into Agentforce agent configuration

sap.notes: SAP GRC provides authorization governance. Autonomy tier concepts for AI agents are not yet native.

microsoft.notes: Agent autonomy controls and guardrails built into Copilot Studio configuration

google.notes: Autonomy controls within Agentspace; comprehensive tier management framework still developing

aws.notes: Guardrails and action controls in Bedrock Agents; comprehensive tier management framework still developing

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No formal autonomy definitions; all AI actions require manual oversight
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic autonomy tiers defined per use case
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Granular autonomy policies per action type, risk level, and domain with real-time enforcement
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Dynamic autonomy that self-adjusts based on agent performance history and situational risk assessment

l2_capabilities.name: Escalation protocol management
l2_capabilities.description: Configurable escalation rules determining when and how agent actions are routed to human review based on confidence, value, and risk thresholds

adobe.notes: Human approval steps in journeys and agent flows; structured escalation framework still emerging

salesforce.notes: Human escalation controls native to Agentforce with Flow-based routing

sap.notes: SAP Build supports escalation workflows. Not purpose-built for AI agent escalation protocols.

microsoft.notes: Human escalation and approval flows native to Copilot Studio and Power Automate

google.notes: Escalation within Agentspace; structured framework still developing

aws.notes: Human approval through Step Functions; structured escalation framework still developing

maturity_indicators.stage_1: All exceptions require manual intervention with no defined escalation paths
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic escalation rules based on fixed thresholds
maturity_indicators.stage_3: AI-informed escalation with confidence, value, and risk-based routing to appropriate human reviewers
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Predictive escalation that pre-routes complex scenarios before they reach decision boundaries

l2_capabilities.name: Progressive autonomy management
l2_capabilities.description: Framework for expanding agent autonomy over time based on demonstrated performance, with rollback capabilities if performance degrades

adobe.notes: No native progressive autonomy framework; requires enterprise governance design

salesforce.notes: Progressive trust within Agentforce; formal autonomy expansion framework still emerging

sap.notes: No progressive autonomy management framework for AI agents.

microsoft.notes: Progressive trust within Copilot; formal autonomy expansion framework still emerging

google.notes: No progressive autonomy framework; requires enterprise governance design

aws.notes: No progressive autonomy framework; requires enterprise governance design

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Fixed autonomy levels with no mechanism for expansion
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Periodic manual review of autonomy boundaries
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Performance-based autonomy expansion with automated evaluation and governed boundary adjustment
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Self-calibrating autonomy with continuous trust scoring and automated rollback on performance degradation

l1_components.name: Decision audit & compliance
l1_components.description: Infrastructure creating auditable records of agent decisions and ensuring regulatory compliance

l2_capabilities.name: Decision logging & traceability
l2_capabilities.description: Comprehensive logging of every agent decision capturing inputs, context retrieved, reasoning chain, action taken, and outcome

adobe.notes: Audit logging within AEP; comprehensive cross-platform decision traceability requires integration

salesforce.notes: Comprehensive audit through Shield with Trust Layer decision logging

sap.notes: SAP GRC provides comprehensive audit logging and decision traceability across enterprise processes.

microsoft.notes: Comprehensive audit logging across Microsoft ecosystem

google.notes: Comprehensive audit logging across all GCP services

aws.notes: Comprehensive audit logging across all AWS services through CloudTrail

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No systematic logging of AI decisions
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic action logging per platform
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Comprehensive cross-platform decision logging capturing full reasoning chain for every agent action
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Real-time decision audit with on-demand reasoning trace and automated compliance verification

l2_capabilities.name: Regulatory compliance framework
l2_capabilities.description: Industry-specific compliance monitoring ensuring agent actions adhere to HIPAA, SR 11-7, CMS, state DOI, GDPR, and other regulatory requirements

adobe.notes: Data governance and privacy controls in AEP; industry-specific regulatory compliance (SR 11-7, HIPAA) requires additional frameworks

salesforce.notes: Audit and compliance within Salesforce; industry-specific AI regulatory frameworks require additional governance

sap.notes: SAP GRC is industry-leading for regulatory compliance frameworks across global jurisdictions.

microsoft.notes: Compliance management tooling; AI-specific regulatory frameworks require additional configuration

google.notes: Compliance controls for regulated workloads; AI-specific regulatory frameworks require additional configuration

aws.notes: Compliance monitoring through Config and Security Hub; AI-specific regulatory frameworks require additional configuration

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No AI-specific regulatory compliance framework
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Manual compliance checks against industry requirements
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Automated compliance monitoring aligned with industry-specific frameworks (HIPAA, SR 11-7, CMS, GDPR)
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous compliance agents adapting governance rules as regulations evolve across jurisdictions

l2_capabilities.name: Cross-platform governance enforcement
l2_capabilities.description: Consistent policy enforcement across all platforms ensuring governance rules apply regardless of which system an agent operates within

adobe.notes: Adobe governance applies within Adobe ecosystem; cross-platform policy enforcement requires enterprise governance layer

salesforce.notes: Strong governance within Salesforce ecosystem; cross-platform enforcement requires integration

sap.notes: SAP GRC provides governance enforcement within SAP landscape. Cross-platform enforcement requires integration.

microsoft.notes: Policy enforcement within Azure/M365; cross-platform enforcement requires integration

google.notes: Policy enforcement within GCP; cross-platform enforcement requires integration

aws.notes: Policy enforcement within AWS; cross-platform enforcement requires integration

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Governance applied inconsistently across platforms
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Platform-specific governance with manual coordination
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Unified governance policies enforced consistently across all platforms regardless of where agents operate
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Self-enforcing cross-platform governance that automatically extends to new platforms and agent deployments

l1_components.name: Cost & performance governance
l1_components.description: Monitoring and control of agentic operations costs and performance

l2_capabilities.name: Inference cost governance
l2_capabilities.description: Tracking and enforcement of inference spend against value generated at agent, workflow, and micro-journey levels

adobe.notes: No native inference cost governance; requires FinOps tooling and integration

salesforce.notes: Usage reporting within Einstein; granular inference cost governance requires additional tooling

sap.notes: BTP provides resource metering; AI Core tracks inference usage. Not unified inference cost governance.

microsoft.notes: Granular cost tracking across all Azure AI and compute workloads

google.notes: Detailed cost tracking with billing export to BigQuery for granular analysis

aws.notes: Detailed cost tracking with Cost and Usage Reports and budget enforcement

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No visibility into AI inference costs
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Platform-level cost reporting with monthly reviews
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Granular cost tracking per agent, workflow, and micro-journey with value-based budget enforcement
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Autonomous cost optimization with real-time spend-to-value monitoring and automatic routing adjustments

l2_capabilities.name: Agent performance monitoring
l2_capabilities.description: Continuous monitoring of agent quality, accuracy, and effectiveness with automated degradation detection and intervention triggers

adobe.notes: Platform-level performance monitoring; agent-specific quality monitoring across platforms requires additional tooling

salesforce.notes: Agent performance monitoring within Agentforce; cross-platform monitoring requires integration

sap.notes: No native agent performance monitoring framework. General application monitoring via Cloud ALM.

microsoft.notes: Infrastructure performance monitoring; agent-specific quality monitoring requires configuration

google.notes: Model monitoring through Vertex AI; agent-specific quality monitoring requires configuration

aws.notes: Model monitoring through SageMaker; agent-specific quality monitoring requires configuration

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No systematic agent performance monitoring
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic accuracy and response time tracking
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Continuous performance monitoring with automated degradation detection, root cause analysis, and intervention
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Predictive performance management detecting degradation patterns before they impact outcomes

l2_capabilities.name: SLA & service quality management
l2_capabilities.description: Service level monitoring for agent response times, resolution rates, and customer satisfaction with escalation when thresholds are breached

adobe.notes: No native agentic SLA management; requires enterprise service management integration

salesforce.notes: Service SLA management in Service Cloud; agentic SLA management across all functions is emerging

sap.notes: SAP Cloud ALM provides SLA monitoring for applications. Not agent-specific service quality management.

microsoft.notes: SLA management within Service; cross-function agentic SLA management requires additional tooling

google.notes: No agentic SLA management; partner required

aws.notes: No agentic SLA management; partner required

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No SLAs defined for agent-driven processes
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic response time SLAs for customer-facing agents
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Comprehensive SLA framework covering response time, resolution quality, and satisfaction with automated escalation
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Self-adjusting SLAs that adapt targets based on workload, complexity, and customer value

l1_components.name: Security & access governance
l1_components.description: Identity, access, and security controls governing who and what can act within the architecture

l2_capabilities.name: Agent identity & access management
l2_capabilities.description: Authentication and authorization framework governing which agents can access which systems, data, and actions

adobe.notes: Adobe identity and access within the ecosystem; cross-platform agent IAM requires enterprise identity infrastructure

salesforce.notes: Enterprise-grade identity and access management within Salesforce ecosystem

sap.notes: SAP Identity Authentication and GRC provide enterprise-grade identity and access management.

microsoft.notes: Enterprise-grade identity platform with conditional access and zero-trust framework

google.notes: Enterprise IAM with fine-grained access controls and workload identity federation

aws.notes: Enterprise IAM with fine-grained policies, SSO, and multi-account governance

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Agents use shared credentials with no individual identity
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic agent authentication with platform-level permissions
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Fine-grained agent identity and access management with role-based permissions and cross-platform governance
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Zero-trust agent identity with dynamic permission adjustment based on context and risk assessment

l2_capabilities.name: Data access governance
l2_capabilities.description: Fine-grained data access controls ensuring agents only access the data they are authorized to use for their specific function

adobe.notes: Attribute-based access control and data governance policies in AEP; strong within Adobe ecosystem

salesforce.notes: Field-level security, data classification, and access governance across the platform

sap.notes: SAP GRC and Data Custodian provide comprehensive data access governance and data sovereignty controls.

microsoft.notes: Fine-grained data access governance through Purview with Azure role-based access control

google.notes: Fine-grained data access governance through IAM policies, Dataplex governance, and VPC Service Controls

aws.notes: Fine-grained data access through Lake Formation; PII detection through Macie; IAM policies

maturity_indicators.stage_1: Agents access data through unrestricted database connections
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic data access controls at the platform level
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Fine-grained data access governance ensuring agents access only authorized data for their specific function
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Dynamic data access with context-aware permissions that adapt based on task, customer consent, and risk

l2_capabilities.name: Security monitoring & threat detection
l2_capabilities.description: Real-time monitoring for anomalous agent behavior, unauthorized access attempts, and potential security threats across the agentic ecosystem

adobe.notes: Activity monitoring within Adobe ecosystem; cross-platform security monitoring requires SIEM integration

salesforce.notes: Comprehensive security monitoring and threat detection within Salesforce ecosystem

sap.notes: SAP Enterprise Threat Detection provides security monitoring for SAP landscapes. Not AI-agent-specific.

microsoft.notes: Enterprise security monitoring and threat detection through Sentinel SIEM and Defender XDR

google.notes: Enterprise security monitoring through Security Command Center with Chronicle SIEM

aws.notes: Comprehensive security: GuardDuty for threat detection, Security Hub for posture, Detective for investigation

maturity_indicators.stage_1: No agent-specific security monitoring
maturity_indicators.stage_2: Basic anomaly detection on agent activity logs
maturity_indicators.stage_3: Real-time security monitoring for anomalous agent behavior, unauthorized access, and threat detection
maturity_indicators.stage_4: Predictive security with autonomous threat detection and automated containment of compromised agents
